message.content:
Try HARD to REFUTE this proposed lever for improving a RACE score. Default to refuted=true when uncertain.

LEVER: Ship-blocker scrub: delete pipeline artifacts that self-incriminate
TARGETS: readability (0.14) / S1 Overall Structure w=0.20; S2 Cohesion w=0.15; L1 Language Clarity w=0.20; F1 Formatting w=0.10 — plus spillover to insight w=0.25 Critical Synthesis (the 'No contradictions were detected by the pipeline' confession) (weight 0.14)
CHANGE: Post-compose sanitizer in the flywheel compose stage: (1) strip all 25 '(also mirrored)' markers from the body; (2) rewrite the 5 dangling section openers (Intro 'Within this framework...', Theory 'This framework...', GenAI 'The study also documents...', the verbless fragment opening Cross-Study Synthesis, 'By contrast' opening Additional Corroborated Findings) so each section opens with a topic sentence; (3) delete the meta boilerplate opener ('Every quantitative claim is span-grounded...') and rewrite Limitations to drop pipeline telemetry language ('No contradictions were detected by the pipeline', 'The telemetry block does not surface...'); (4) merge 'Additional Corroborated Findings' into thematic sections so nothing follows the Conclusion; (5) break each single-paragraph section into 3-5 paragraphs; (6) add the missing final period. NOTE: the brief's 'ings in the last two years' mid-word bug and fact_dedup cross-references do NOT exist in Rank10 — do not chase them.
CLAIMED GAIN: +0.010 to +0.015 (readability 0.377 → ~0.46; ceiling limited by 0.14 weight but this is our worst dim and the fixes are deterministic)
CLAIMED FAILURE MODE: Barely can. Worst case the paragraph-splitting is arbitrary and S2 doesn't move; the marker/fragment deletions are pure upside. Risk of over-scrubbing the Limitations section into vacuity — keep a 2-sentence honest scope note.

ATTACK IT THROUGH THIS LENS:
MEASUREMENT: would this lever actually move the RACE judge, or only an internal proxy? Recall RACE is reference-based: Overall = target/(target+reference). A change that improves our report but that the JUDGE cannot see, or that the reference ALSO does well, yields ~0 points.

Context: our report /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md scored 0.4313. Reference /home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md = 0.5000 by definition. SOTA = 0.5265.
Read the files if you need to check a claim. Be adversarial, concrete, and brief.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

TASK 72 PROMPT:
Please write a literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market. Focus on how AI, as a key driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, is causing significant disruptions and affecting various industries. Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles.

DIMENSION WEIGHTS: {"readability": 0.14, "insight": 0.32, "comprehensiveness": 0.29, "instruction_following": 0.25}

=== COMPREHENSIVENESS (weight 0.29) ===
- [w=0.10] Grounding in AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Assesses whether the review adequately defines AI within the context of the 4IR and explains its fundamental role as a driver of labor market restructuring. This contextual framing is crucial for understanding the scope and nature of the impacts discussed.
- [w=0.25] Breadth of Labor Market Restructuring Dimensions Covered :: Evaluates the extent to which the review addresses the multifaceted nature of AI's impact on the labor market, including job creation, job displacement, job transformation, changes in skill demands, wage effects, and productivity changes.
- [w=0.25] Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis :: Checks if the review examines the restructuring impact of AI across a diverse range of relevant industries, highlighting both common trends and sector-specific nuances. The task explicitly requires focusing on 'various industries.'
- [w=0.15] Exploration of AI's Disruptive Character and Scale :: Assesses whether the review sufficiently explores the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI, including the magnitude, speed, and transformative potential of these changes on labor market structures and dynamics.
- [w=0.15] Depth and Representativeness of Literature Synthesized :: Evaluates if the review synthesizes a broad and representative selection of current, high-quality academic literature (from the specified journal articles), covering the main research themes, findings, and ongoing debates regarding AI's impact on the labor market. This ensures the review's content is built upon a solid and comprehensive foundation of existing knowledge.
- [w=0.10] Balanced Discussion of AI's Labor Market Impacts :: Assesses whether the review presents a nuanced perspective by discussing both the challenges (e.g., displacement, skill gaps, inequality) and opportunities (e.g., new job creation, productivity gains, enhanced work quality) arising from AI's influence on the labor market.

=== INSIGHT (weight 0.32) ===
- [w=0.25] Analytical Depth in Characterizing AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring Mechanisms :: Assesses if the review moves beyond superficial descriptions of labor market changes to deeply analyze the underlying *mechanisms* (e.g., task automation, skill augmentation, job creation/destruction dynamics, organizational adaptation) through which AI specifically reshapes job roles, skill requirements, and overall labor market structures.
- [w=0.25] Critical Synthesis and Nuanced Evaluation of AI's Disruptive Impacts Across Industries :: Evaluates the review's ability to critically synthesize diverse findings from high-quality journal articles to present a nuanced understanding of AI's 'significant disruptions.' This includes identifying patterns of impact, variations across different industries, and areas of consensus, debate, or uncertainty in the literature, rather than merely cataloging effects.
- [w=0.15] Insightful Integration of AI's Role within the Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) Context :: Measures how effectively the review articulates and leverages the 4IR framework to provide a deeper, more contextualized understanding of the *nature, scale, and interconnectedness* of AI's transformative power on the labor market, rather than treating AI's impact in isolation or merely mentioning the 4IR superficially.
- [w=0.20] Identification and Articulation of Emergent Themes, Theoretical Linkages, or Novel Perspectives :: Assesses whether the review transcends simple summarization to identify and clearly articulate overarching emergent themes, significant theoretical linkages between concepts, or potentially novel perspectives derived from the synthesis of the reviewed literature on AI's labor market impact.
- [w=0.15] Value and Foresight in Delineating Implications and Future Research Agendas :: Evaluates the review's capacity to distill strategically valuable implications (e.g., for policy, education, workforce adaptation strategies) and to clearly delineate critical research gaps or promising future research agendas based on the comprehensive synthesis of the current state of knowledge.

=== INSTRUCTION_FOLLOWING (weight 0.25) ===
- [w=0.10] Adherence to 'Literature Review' Format and Purpose :: Assesses if the article is structured and executed as a literature review, meaning it synthesizes and discusses existing published research on the topic, rather than presenting new empirical findings or being solely an opinion piece.
- [w=0.20] Consistent Focus on 'Restructuring Impact of AI on the Labor Market' :: Evaluates whether the article's content remains consistently and primarily focused on AI's restructuring effects on the labor market, as per the core task instruction, without significant digressions into unrelated AI applications or general economic theory not tied to labor impacts.
- [w=0.15] Integration of 'AI as a Key Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' Theme :: Checks if the review explicitly incorporates and discusses AI within the specific context of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, as mandated by the task's focus requirements.
- [w=0.15] Explicit Addressal of AI-Driven 'Significant Disruptions' in the Labor Market :: Assesses whether the review specifically addresses and elaborates on the 'significant disruptions' caused by AI in the labor market, fulfilling another key focus point of the task.
- [w=0.15] Coverage of AI's Impact on 'Various Industries' :: Evaluates if the review discusses the impact of AI on the labor market across a spectrum of 'various industries,' rather than limiting its scope to a single industry or providing only a generic overview, as per the instruction.
- [w=0.15] Exclusive Citation of 'High-Quality Journal Articles' :: Verifies that all cited sources are academic, peer-reviewed journal articles (or meet a clear standard of 'high-quality' if defined contextually, e.g., leading journals), and that other forms of literature (e.g., books, conference proceedings, news articles, blogs, non-peer-reviewed reports) are not cited, adhering to the 'only cites high-quality... journal articles' constraint.
- [w=0.10] Exclusive Citation of 'English-Language' Journal Articles :: Ensures that all journal articles cited in the review are originally published in English or are established English translations, strictly adhering to the language constraint for sources.

=== READABILITY (weight 0.14) ===
- [w=0.20] L1: Language Clarity, Precision, and Academic Tone :: Evaluates the accuracy and fluency of sentence construction, grammar, spelling, and punctuation. Assesses the precise, consistent, and appropriate use of technical terminology related to AI, labor markets, the Fourth Industrial Revolution, and specific industries. Ensures an objective, formal academic tone suitable for a literature review. For example, terms like 'skill-biased technical change' or specific AI techniques should be used correctly and, if necessary, briefly contextualized for a broader academic audience.
- [w=0.20] S1: Overall Structure and Logical Organization :: Assesses the coherence and logic of the review's macro-structure. This includes a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map; logically sequenced thematic sections with informative headings that reflect the facets of AI's impact on labor (e.g., by industry, skill level, type of disruption); and a conclusion that effectively synthesizes the main themes from the literature and perhaps points to gaps or future research directions.
- [w=0.15] S2: Paragraph Cohesion and Transitions :: Evaluates if each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea or theme derived from the literature, with sentences flowing logically within the paragraph. Assesses the effectiveness of transitional phrases, sentences, and logical connectors in linking ideas smoothly between paragraphs and across different sections of the review, ensuring a coherent narrative thread.
- [w=0.15] P1: Clarity and Synthesis in Presenting Sourced Information :: Assesses how clearly, concisely, and accurately key arguments, findings, theories, and methodologies from the cited journal articles are presented. Evaluates the effectiveness of synthesizing information from multiple sources to build a coherent understanding of topics (e.g., AI's effect on job displacement vs. creation, or changes in skill demands across industries), rather than just serially summarizing individual papers. Checks for appropriate information density and avoidance of redundancy.
- [w=0.10] D1: Clarity of Data/Evidence Referenced or Summarized :: Evaluates the clarity with which quantitative or qualitative data, empirical findings, or specific evidence from cited studies (e.g., statistics on automation rates, survey results on worker sentiment) are described, interpreted, or summarized within the review. If the review itself uses tables or figures to compile or synthesize information from sources (e.g., a summary table of key studies and their findings), assesses their clarity, labeling, and effectiveness in aiding comprehension.
- [w=0.10] F1: Formatting, Layout, and Visual Consistency :: Evaluates the use of consistent and professional formatting (e.g., font choice and size, spacing, margins, heading styles) that enhances readability. Assesses the overall visual layout for ease of navigation and reduced reader fatigue, including clear paragraph breaks. Also considers the visual consistency and unobtrusive integration of in-text citations and the clarity of the bibliography's formatting, which contributes to the academic presentation.
- [w=0.10] A1: Audience Adaptation and Explanation of Terms :: Assesses if the language, level of detail, and style of argumentation are appropriate for an academic audience interested in AI's impact on the labor market. Evaluates whether potentially ambiguous or highly specialized terms are sufficiently contextualized or briefly explained to ensure understanding by readers who may not be experts in every specific sub-domain discussed (e.g., specific AI algorithms or niche economic theories).
===
# POLARIS BENCHMARK TARGET — the real scoreboard (from web search 2026-07-11)

## What we must beat
**FS-Researcher** (arXiv:2602.01566, ACL 2026) — "Test-Time Scaling for Long-Horizon Research
Tasks with File-System-Based Agents". A file-system dual-agent (Context Builder + Report Writer)
that scales research BEYOND the context window via a persistent knowledge base. This is the same
thesis as POLARIS (process ALL 329 baskets (current s2s3 iter-7 count; 346 was the stale iter-6 count), not one context window). It claims **SOTA report
quality** across backbones. Code: github.com/Ignoramus0817/FS-Researcher.

## The benchmarks it is scored on (both open-ended report quality)
1. **DeepResearch Bench** (arXiv:2506.11763) — 100 PhD-level research tasks.
   - Scorer: **RACE** — reference-based LLM judge, 4 dimensions with per-task DYNAMIC weights:
     Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, Readability.
     Compares the target report against a high-quality REFERENCE report.
   - Download: `github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench` — tasks at
     `data/prompt_data/query.jsonl` (100 tasks); HF dataset `muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Dataset`.
   - Leaderboard (ChatGPT/Gemini deep-research RACE scores) is on HuggingFace.
2. **DeepConsult** — open-ended consulting-style report quality (pairwise/quality judge).
3. (also referenced for the agent side: BrowseComp, BFCL, TAU-Bench, MCP Agent Bench.)

Related: **DeepResearch Bench II** (arXiv:2601.08536) — rubric-from-expert-report diagnosis
(the DRB-II wrapper already stubbed in the compete wheel).

## What this means for POLARIS
- **RACE is the correct instrument** — confirms the compete wheel's direction; DeepTRACE (faithfulness)
  is a SEPARATE, complementary axis, not the mission's quality axis.
- "Beat FS-Researcher" is now RUNNABLE: run POLARIS on the DeepResearch Bench 100 tasks, score with
  RACE against the reference reports, compare to FS-Researcher + the ChatGPT/Gemini leaderboard.
- The two build wheels serve this end:
  - **OUTLINE** → depth + compute-and-prove drives Comprehensiveness + Insight (RACE dims 1-2) and
    the faithfulness moat (DeepTRACE) that frontier products lack.
  - **COMPOSE** → renders all 329 baskets (current s2s3 iter-7 count; 346 was the stale iter-6 count) fast + faithful so nothing is dropped for latency =
    Comprehensiveness + Readability, at full corpus depth.
- Open gap: we still have NO FS-Researcher report artifact locally; the benchmark download closes it
  (we can run POLARIS on the same 100 tasks and score head-to-head).

# A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market

This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.

## Introduction and Scope of the Evidence

Within this framework, automation generates a displacement effect that reduces labor's share of value added, countered by a reinstatement effect as new tasks reinstate labor into a broader range of activities, and the net impact depends on the relative magnitudes of these opposing forces.[1] The contemporary context for these dynamics is the Fourth Industrial Revolution, characterized by a fusion of technologies blurring the lines between the physical, digital, and biological spheres and evolving at an exponential rather than linear pace.[2] Within this revolution, AI and smart robotics are described as emerging technologies that dramatically change not only productivity and the ways of work but also the types and number of available jobs.[3] The Fourth Industrial Revolution is also framed as a paradigm of cyber-physical systems constituted by general-purpose technologies applied across multiple industries, including artificial intelligence, blockchain, genomics, and the Internet of Things.[4] Empirically, a study tracking AI adoption from 2010 to 2023 found that when AI can perform most tasks comprising a particular job, the share of people in that role within a company falls by about 14 percent, but when AI's impact is concentrated in just a few tasks, employment in that role can grow.[5] Even workers in high-wa
...

input.command:
R=/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md; grep -c "also mirrored" $R; grep -n "^#" $R; echo "=== para counts per section"; awk '/^## /{s=$0} /^$/{next} {print}' $R | head -0; python3 - <<'EOF'
import re
t=open('/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md').read()
secs=re.split(r'\n(?=## )',t)
for s in secs:
    head=s.split('\n')[0][:60]
    paras=[p for p in s.split('\n\n')[1:] if p.strip() and not p.startswith('#')]
    words=len(s.split())
    print(f"{head!r:65} paras={len(paras)} words={words}")
EOF

content.content:
## Limitations

Limitations: The corpus skews heavily toward secondary and synthesized sources, with only 6% of materials classified as T1 primary studies, while T4 and T6 tiers each account for 21% and a further 15% of sources remain of UNKNOWN tier, indicating substantial gaps in high-certainty empirical coverage. No contradictions were detected by the pipeline, so no cross-source disagreement on magnitude, direction, or endpoint is asserted. The telemetry block does not surface an explicit date range for the corpus, which limits the ability to characterize the evidence horizon or identify temporal gaps in source coverage. These distributional and provenance constraints mean the pipeline's synthesis capacity is weighted toward interpretation-layer material rather than original experimental or observational data.

input.command: for d in /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/*compose*/report.md; do echo "== $d"; grep -c "span-grounded" $d; grep -o "also mirrored" $d|wc -l; grep -c "detected by the pipeline" $d; done 2>/dev/null

content.text:
**REFUTED.** Four independent kills, all from measurement.

**1. The claimed gain is below the measured noise floor.** `SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:7,33`: "Rank9 and Rank10 are the SAME effective config => their 0.0158 gap IS the noise floor." Claimed gain is +0.010 to +0.015 — entirely inside ±0.016. And 0.4313 is the *lucky* draw of that pair (0.4155/0.4313). You cannot detect this lever with a single re-run, and the baseline it's measured against is the high draw.

**2. Cross-arm data shows the artifacts do not drive readability.** Marker counts vs readability comparative: Rank7 = 27 markers → 0.3379; Rank9 = 30 → 0.3588; Rank10 = 25 → 0.3774. Zero correlation. Every arm carries the same `span-grounded` boilerplate opener. Meanwhile CTRL (3,343 words) scores the *best* readability (0.4008) with the same pipeline artifacts, and readability collapsed as sections grew. Readability tracks **section length / monolithic density**, not the markers/fragments/telemetry. The lever is targeting a variable already shown to be non-causal.

**3. Arithmetic ceiling.** readability 0.3774 → full parity with the reference (0.50) is worth `0.14 × 0.1226 = +0.0172` total, ever. The lever claims +0.010–0.015 = **58–87% of the entire remaining readability headroom** from a cosmetic sanitizer. In raw terms 0.377→0.46 requires our raw score to go from 0.61× the reference to 0.85× — a +41% raw readability jump from deleting 25 parentheticals and adding a period. Not credible.

**4. It leaves the actual S1/S2/P1 deficit untouched, and item (5) is the base case, not the worst case.** Measured structure: our report is **11 flat H2s, every one a single 537–990-word paragraph**, prose = one-sentence-per-citation chains ([1] … [2] … [3] …). The reference is 8 numbered H2s × 3–4 **H3 subsections**, 2–6 paragraphs each, 130–780 words per subsection. So:
- **S1 (w=0.20, the largest readability sub-criterion)** asks for hierarchical thematic organization — the sanitizer adds no H3 hierarchy, so S1 stays capped.
- **P1 (w=0.15)** explicitly penalizes "just serially summarizing individual papers" — that's exactly our prose, and the sanitizer does not touch a single sentence of it.
- **S2 (w=0.15)** requires "each paragraph focuses on a single, clear idea." Mechanically chopping a serial citation chain into 3–5 chunks produces paragraphs with no controlling idea. S2 does not move; a judge reading the result can see the chunking is arbitrary. The lever files this as "worst case"; it is the *expected* case.
- **F1 (w=0.10)** is the only thing genuinely fixed: 0.10 × 0.14 = **1.4% of total weight**, max ≈ +0.007 even at full swing.

**5. The insight spillover is fabricated.** Critical Synthesis (0.25 × 0.32 = 8% of total) is scored on whether the report *surfaces* debates, patterns, and uncertainty. Deleting "No contradictions were detected by the pipeline" removes the *admission*, not the *deficiency* — the 650-word "Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions" section still contains no cross-source disagreement analysis. RACE judges score substance present, not confessions absent.

**6. Item (3) is a net-negative risk that the lever misdiagnoses.** The "meta boilerplate opener" it wants deleted contains the report's *only* organizational map ("an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence, a cross-study synthesis…, a closing discussion") — precisely what S1 rewards ("a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map"). Only the final sentence is self-incriminating. Deleting the paragraph loses S1 points.

**Opportunity cost:** readability is the *lowest-weighted* dim (0.14). Comprehensiveness (0.29 @ 0.4549) and Insight (0.32 @ 0.4238) hold 3–5× more headroom per point of comparative gain. "Our worst dim" is the wrong selection criterion.

Relevant files: `/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/outputs/rank10_sections_compose/report.md`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/RACE_REFERENCE_task72.md`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md`, `/home/polaris/polaris_project/race_task72_criteria.txt`

input.lever: Ship-blocker scrub: delete pipeline artifacts that self-incriminate
input.reasoning:
MEASUREMENT KILLS IT FOUR WAYS.

(1) BELOW THE NOISE FLOOR. SOTA_REVIEW_BRIEF.md:7,33: Rank9 and Rank10 are the same effective config; their 0.0158 gap IS the measured judge noise (0.4155 vs 0.4313). The claim is +0.010 to +0.015 — entirely inside +/-0.016. Worse, 0.4313 is the HIGH draw of that noise pair, so the "gain" is unmeasurable against an already-inflated baseline. A single re-run cannot attribute anything to this lever.

(2) CROSS-ARM DATA SHOWS THE ARTIFACTS ARE NON-CAUSAL. Counted markers per arm vs measured readability comparative: Rank7 = 27 markers -> 0.3379; Rank9 = 30 -> 0.3588; Rank10 = 25 -> 0.3774. No correlation. All arms (incl. rank6/7/9) carry the identical 'span-grounded' boilerplate opener. Meanwhile CTRL baseline (3,343 words) scores the BEST readability (0.4008) carrying the same pipeline artifacts, and readability fell as sections grew. Readability tracks section length / monolithic-paragraph density, NOT the markers, fragments, or telemetry sentences the sanitizer removes. The lever targets a variable the data already shows is not driving the score.

(3) ARITHMETIC CEILING. Readability comparative 0.3774 -> full parity with the reference (0.50) is worth at most 0.14 * 0.1226 = +0.0172 total, ever. The lever claims +0.010-0.015 = 58-87% of ALL remaining readability headroom from a cosmetic post-process. In raw terms, 0.377 -> 0.46 requires target raw to go from 0.61x reference to 0.85x reference — a +41% raw readability jump from deleting 25 parentheticals, fixing 5 openers, and adding a period. Not credible. F1 (the one sub-criterion genuinely fixed) is w=0.10 of a w=0.14 dim = 1.4% of total; even a perfect F1 swing caps around +0.007.

(4) THE REAL DEFICIT IS UNTOUCHED, AND ITEM (5) IS THE BASE CASE NOT THE WORST CASE. Measured: our report = 11 FLAT H2 sections, every one a SINGLE 537-990-word paragraph, prose written as one-sentence-per-citation chains ([1]...[2]...[3]...). The reference = 8 numbered H2s x 3-4 H3 SUBSECTIONS, 2-6 paragraphs each. So S1 (w=0.20, largest readability sub-weight, explicitly rewards hierarchical thematic organization + organizational map) stays capped because the sanitizer adds no hierarchy. P1 (w=0.15) explicitly penalizes "just serially summarizing individual papers" — exactly our prose — and the sanitizer edits not one sentence of it. S2 (w=0.15) requires each paragraph to carry a single controlling idea; mechanically chopping a serial citation chain into 3-5 chunks yields paragraphs with no controlling idea, visibly arbitrary to the judge. The lever files this as "worst case"; it is the expected case.

(5) THE INSIGHT SPILLOVER IS FABRICATED. Critical Synthesis (0.25 x 0.32 = 8% of total) is scored on whether the report SURFACES debates/patterns/uncertainty. Deleting "No contradictions were detected by the pipeline" removes the admission, not the deficiency — the 650-word "Cross-Study Synthesis and Contradictions" section still contains no cross-source disagreement analysis. Judges score substance present, not confessions absent. Zero expected insight points.

(6) ITEM (3) IS NET-NEGATIVE AS SPECIFIED. The "meta boilerplate opener" it deletes contains the report's ONLY organizational map ("an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections..., a cross-study synthesis..., a closing discussion") — precisely what S1 rewards ("a clear introduction defining the review's scope, purpose, and organizational map"). Only the last sentence is self-incriminating; deleting the paragraph loses S1 points.

(7) OPPORTUNITY COST. Readability is the LOWEST-weighted dim (0.14). Comprehensiveness (0.29 @ 0.4549) and Insight (0.32 @ 0.4238) hold 3-5x more headroom per unit of comparative gain. "It's our worst dim" is the wrong selection criterion under weighted scoring.
input.salvage: Do the free subset as hygiene, NOT as a scoring lever, and do not book points for it: strip the 25 '(also mirrored)' markers, add the missing final period, rewrite the 5 dangling section openers into topic sentences, and delete ONLY the three telemetry sentences from Limitations (keep a 2-sentence honest scope note). KEEP the roadmap paragraph — delete only its final 'span-grounded' sentence, since the roadmap is the only S1 organizational map we have. Expected value ~+0.003, under noise, but never negative. DROP items (4) and (5) as a sanitizer: arbitrary paragraph chopping and wholesale relocation of the 673-word 'Additional Corroborated Findings' leftovers can degrade S2 in the receiving sections. The real readability lever is a COMPOSE-STAGE change, not a post-process: emit H3 subsections under each H2 (mirroring the reference's 8x3-4 hierarchy, 130-780 words each) and write synthesis-mode paragraphs that group 2-4 sources per claim instead of one sentence per citation. That attacks S1/S2/P1 at the source and would spill into Insight — but it is a different, larger lever and must be sized and measured on its own (with >=2 runs, given the +/-0.016 noise floor).
